lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: Kill the poys and the luggage! ’tis expressly against the law of

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: arms: ’tis as arrant a piece of knavery, mark you now, as

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: can be offer’t; in your conscience, now, is it not?

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: ’Tis certain there’s not a boy left alive; and the cowardly rascals

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: that ran from the battle ha’ done this slaughter: besides, they have

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: burned and carried away all that was in the king’s tent; wherefore

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: the king, most worthily, hath caused every soldier to cut his prisoner’s

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: throat. O, ’tis a gallant king!

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: Ay, he was porn at Monmouth, Captain Gower. What call you the

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: town’s name where Alexander the Pig was born!

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: Alexander the Great.

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: Why, I pray you, is not pig great? the pig, or the

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: great, or the mighty, or the huge, or the magnanimous, are all

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: one reckonings, save the phrase is a little variations.

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: I think Alexander the Great was born in Macedon: his father was

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: called Philip of Macedon, as I take it.

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: I think it is in Macedon where Alexander is porn. I tell

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: you, captain, if you look in the maps of the ’orld, I

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: warrant you sall find, in the comparisons between Macedon and Monmouth, that

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: the situations, look you, is both alike. There is a river in

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: Macedon; and there is also moreover a river at Monmouth: it is

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: called Wye at Monmouth; but it is out of my prains what

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: is the name of the other river; but ’tis all one, ’tis

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: alike as my fingers is to my fingers, and there is salmons

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: in both. If you mark Alexander’s life well, Harry of Monmouth’s life

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: is come after it indifferent well; for there is figures in all

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: things. Alexander, God knows, and you know, in his rages, and his

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: furies, and his wraths, and his cholers, and his moods, and his

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: displeasures, and his indignations, and also being a little intoxicates in his

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: prains, did, in his ales and his angers, look you, kill his

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: best friend, Cleitus.

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: Our king is not like him in that: he never killed any of his friends.

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: It is not well done, mark you now, to take the tales

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: out of my mouth, ere it is made and finished. I speak

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: but in the figures and comparisons of it: as Alexander killed his

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: friend Cleitus, being in his ales and his cups; so also Harry

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: Monmouth, being in his right wits and his good judgements, turned away

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: the fat knight with the great belly doublet: he was full of

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: jests, and gipes, and knaveries, and mocks; I have forgot his name.

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: Sir John Falstaff.

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: That is he: I’ll tell you there is good men porn at Monmouth.

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: Here comes his majesty.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: I was not angry since I came to France

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Until this instant. Take a trumpet, herald;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Ride thou unto the horsemen on yond hill:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: If they will fight with us, bid them come down,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Or void the field; they do offend our sight:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: If they’ll do neither, we will come to them,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And make them skirr away, as swift as stones

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Enforced from the old Assyrian slings:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Besides, we’ll cut the throats of those we have,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And not a man of them that we shall take

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Shall taste our mercy. Go and tell them so.

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: Here comes the herald of the French, my liege.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: His eyes are humbler than they used to be.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: How now! what means this, herald? know’st thou not

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: That I have fined these bones of mine for ransom?

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Comest thou again for ransom?

lines.speaker: MONTJOY
lines.text: No, great king:

lines.speaker: MONTJOY
lines.text: I come to thee for charitable license,

lines.speaker: MONTJOY
lines.text: That we may wander o’er this bloody field

lines.speaker: MONTJOY
lines.text: To book our dead, and then to bury them;

lines.speaker: MONTJOY
lines.text: To sort our nobles from our common men.

lines.speaker: MONTJOY
lines.text: For many of our princes—woe the while!—

lines.speaker: MONTJOY
lines.text: Lie drown’d and soak’d in mercenary blood;

lines.speaker: MONTJOY
lines.text: So do our vulgar drench their peasant limbs

lines.speaker: MONTJOY
lines.text: In blood of princes; and their wounded steeds

lines.speaker: MONTJOY
lines.text: Fret fetlock deep in gore and with wild rage

lines.speaker: MONTJOY
lines.text: Yerk out their armed heels at their dead masters,

lines.speaker: MONTJOY
lines.text: Killing them twice. O, give us leave, great king,

lines.speaker: MONTJOY
lines.text: To view the field in safety and dispose

lines.speaker: MONTJOY
lines.text: Of their dead bodies!

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: I tell thee truly, herald,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: I know not if the day be ours or no;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: For yet a many of your horsemen peer

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And gallop o’er the field.

lines.speaker: MONTJOY
lines.text: The day is yours.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Praised be God, and not our strength, for it!

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: What is this castle call’d that stands hard by?

lines.speaker: MONTJOY
lines.text: They call it Agincourt.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Then call we this the field of Agincourt.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Fought on the day of Crispin Crispianus.

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: Your grandfather of famous memory, an’t please your majesty, and your great-uncle

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: Edward the Plack Prince of Wales, as I have read in the

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: chronicles, fought a most prave pattle here in France.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: They did, Fluellen.

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: Your majesty says very true: if your majesties is remembered of it,

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: the Welshmen did good service in garden where leeks did grow, wearing

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: leeks in their Monmouth caps; which, your majesty know, to this hour

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: is an honourable badge of the service; and I do believe your

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: majesty takes no scorn to wear the leek upon Saint Tavy’s day.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: I wear it for a memorable honour;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: For I am Welsh, you know, good countryman.

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: All the water in Wye cannot wash your majesty’s Welsh plood out

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: of your pody, I can tell you that: Got pless it and

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: preserve it, as long as it pleases his grace, and his majesty

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: too!

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Thanks, good my countryman.

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: By Jeshu, I am your majesty’s countryman, I care not who know

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: it; I will confess it to all the ’orld: I need not

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: be ashamed of your majesty, praised be God, so long as your

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: majesty is an honest man.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: God keep me so! Our heralds go with him:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Bring me just notice of the numbers dead

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: On both our parts. Call yonder fellow hither.

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: Soldier, you must come to the king.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Soldier, why wearest thou that glove in thy cap?

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: An’t please your majesty, ’tis the gage of one that I should

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: fight withal, if he be alive.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: An Englishman?

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: An’t please your majesty, a rascal that swaggered with me last night;

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: who, if alive and ever dare to challenge this glove, I have

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: sworn to take him a box o’ th’ ear: or if I

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: can see my glove in his cap, which he swore, as he

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: was a soldier, he would wear if alive, I will strike it

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: out soundly.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: What think you, Captain Fluellen? is it fit this soldier keep his oath?

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: He is a craven and a villain else, an’t please your majesty, in my conscience.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: It may be his enemy is a gentlemen of great sort, quite

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: from the answer of his degree.

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: Though he be as good a gentleman as the devil is, as

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: Lucifier and Belzebub himself, it is necessary, look your grace, that he

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: keep his vow and his oath: if he be perjured, see you

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: now, his reputation is as arrant a villain and a Jacksauce, as

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: ever his black shoe trod upon God’s ground and his earth, in

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: my conscience, la!

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Then keep thy vow, sirrah, when thou meetest the fellow.

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: So I will, my liege, as I live.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Who servest thou under?

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: Under Captain Gower, my liege.

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: Gower is a good captain, and is good knowledge and literatured in the wars.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Call him hither to me, soldier.

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: I will, my liege.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Here, Fluellen; wear thou this favour for me and stick it in

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: thy cap: when Alençon and myself were down together, I plucked this

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: glove from his helm: if any man challenge this, he is a

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: friend to Alençon, and an enemy to our person; if thou encounter

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: any such, apprehend him, an thou dost me love.

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: Your grace doo’s me as great honours as can be desired in

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: the hearts of his subjects: I would fain see the man, that

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: has but two legs, that shall find himself aggriefed at this glove;

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: that is all; but I would fain see it once, an please

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: God of his grace that I might see.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Knowest thou Gower?

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: He is my dear friend, an please you.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Pray thee, go seek him, and bring him to my tent.

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: I will fetch him.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: My Lord of Warwick, and my brother Gloucester,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Follow Fluellen closely at the heels:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: The glove which I have given him for a favour

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: May haply purchase him a box o’ th’ ear;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: It is the soldier’s; I by bargain should

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Wear it myself. Follow, good cousin Warwick:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: If that the soldier strike him, as I judge

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: By his blunt bearing he will keep his word,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Some sudden mischief may arise of it;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: For I do know Fluellen valiant

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And, touch’d with choler, hot as gunpowder,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And quickly will return an injury:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Follow, and see there be no harm between them.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Go you with me, uncle of Exeter.

stage_directions.text: Alarum. Enter King Henry and forces; Warwick, Gloucester, Exeter, and others.
Points to Williams. Exeunt Heralds with Montjoy.

glosses.phrase: Kill the poys and the luggage
glosses.definition: 'kill the boys and the [plunder the] luggage' — Fluellen reports the French raid on the English camp, the crime that (in the play's chronology) provides cover for Henry's order to kill prisoners
the accepted rules of war — killing non-combatant boys violated the chivalric code
Gower's interpretation of Henry's order — retaliation for the French killing the camp-boys. The play hedges the timing
born at Monmouth (Welsh pronunciation) — Henry's birthplace, in Wales
Fluellen's blunder for 'Alexander the Big' — he means 'great' but the Welsh word 'pig' slips in; the error becomes the scene's joke
Alexander III of Macedon — classical conqueror; Fluellen's chosen parallel to Henry
the Macedonian capital — Gower's correct version
Philip II — Alexander's father
Fluellen's parallel of Monmouth's River Wye with an unnamed Macedonian river
the river at Monmouth — a real Welsh river
typological parallels in everything — Fluellen's Renaissance rhetoric of correspondences
Alexander's friend and general, killed by Alexander in a drunken rage (328 BCE) — a notorious ancient example of tyrant-error

glosses.phrase: fat knight with the great belly doublet
glosses.definition: Sir John Falstaff — Fluellen's parallel: as Alexander killed Cleitus drunk, Henry 'turned away' Falstaff sober. The rejection at the end of Henry IV Part 2
the plump companion of Henry's wild youth, rejected at Henry's coronation; his death was reported in 2.3
flee swiftly; scatter
the slings of ancient Assyrian warriors — proverbial for speed and force

glosses.phrase: cut the throats of those we have
glosses.definition: Henry renews the threat to kill prisoners — note the double mention; the play makes this choice unavoidable

glosses.phrase: fined these bones of mine for ransom
glosses.definition: paid my ransom with my own bones — Henry's pledge to die on the field, not be ransomed
Montjoy asks permission to bury the French dead — the battle is over
register our dead (in a book)

glosses.phrase: sort our nobles from our common men
glosses.definition: separate the nobles from the common dead for different burials — rank held even in death
the blood of hirelings — a French nobleman has been killed beside commoners
horses' ankles submerged in blood
kick out with their shod hooves
the horses trample their own fallen masters — killing what is already dead
Henry asks the name of the nearest castle — the question that gives the battle its name
Azincourt — a small village near the battlefield; Henry names the battle on the spot
the two Crispin saints (see 4.3) — Henry formally consecrates the day to them
Black Prince of Wales (Fluellen's Welsh pronunciation) — Edward III's son, hero of Crécy
legendary Welsh battlefield where Welsh soldiers wore leeks to identify each other — the origin of the Welsh leek-badge
knitted woollen caps traditionally made at Monmouth — worn by Welsh soldiers
St. David's Day, March 1 — Welsh national day (Fluellen's pronunciation of 'Davy')
Henry's affirmation — he was born at Monmouth and is proud of his Welsh blood
the River Wye — cannot wash out his Welshness
'God bless it' in Fluellen's Welsh accent
by Jesus (Fluellen's Welsh oath)
pledge/glove from Williams; Henry re-opens the 4.1 plot
a slap on the ear (Williams's promised retribution)

glosses.phrase: from the answer of his degree
glosses.definition: socially too high to be challenged by a commoner
Lucifer and Beelzebub — chief devils; Fluellen's rhetorical flourish
impudent fellow — 'Jack' + 'sauce'

glosses.phrase: when Alençon and myself were down together
glosses.definition: Henry had been in single combat with the Duke of Alençon — historically attested; Alençon died
aggrieved, in Fluellen's accent
Fluellen's quick temper — sets up the 4.8 payoff

annotations.title: The French raid on the boys — retroactive justification
annotations.body: Fluellen's opening lines announce that the French have raided the English baggage camp and killed 'the poys' (the boys, in Welsh pronunciation). Gower's immediate interpretive move is to connect this atrocity to Henry's prisoner-killing order of the previous scene: 'the king, most worthily, hath caused every soldier to cut his prisoner's throat. O, 'tis a gallant king!' But the textual chronology is reversed. In the play's spoken sequence, Henry gave the order BEFORE the raid on the boys was reported. Gower is retroactively supplying a just-war justification that the moment of the order did not provide. The play keeps both framings visible. A reader can, with Gower, read the prisoner-killing as legitimate retaliation; a reader can, with the blunt text of 4.6, read it as a tactical decision with independent justification. The scene's structural honesty is that it supplies Gower's reading as reading, not as fact.
sources: Anne Curry, Agincourt: A New History (2005)

annotations.title: Alexander the Pig — the play's shrewdest parallel
annotations.body: Fluellen's comparison of Henry to Alexander the Great is one of the play's most quietly consequential passages. The comedy is Welsh-dialect: 'Alexander the Pig' (for 'Big'), rivers in Macedon and Monmouth, 'salmons in both.' But the parallel Fluellen draws is dangerous. He reminds Gower that Alexander, 'in his rages, and his furies,' killed his best friend Cleitus. Gower objects: Our king is not like Alexander in that; Henry never killed any of his friends. Fluellen corrects him: 'as Alexander killed his friend Cleitus, being in his ales and his cups; so also Harry Monmouth, being in his right wits and his good judgements, turned away the fat knight with the great belly doublet.' The fat knight is Falstaff. Fluellen is saying: Alexander-drunk killed Cleitus; Henry-sober rejected Falstaff; the parallel holds. This is the play's clearest acknowledgement that Henry's rejection of Falstaff was a moral action of the same order as Alexander's murder of his friend. A Welshman in a military camp, in dialect-inflected comic cadence, delivers what is arguably the most damning critical observation the play ventures about Henry.
sources: Harold Bloom, Shakespeare: The Invention of the Human (1998)
A. C. Bradley, 'The Rejection of Falstaff' (1902)

annotations.title: 'We call it Agincourt' — naming the victory
annotations.body: When Montjoy returns with no more ransom-request but a plea for permission to bury the French dead, Henry asks the name of the nearest castle. Montjoy answers: 'Agincourt.' Henry immediately christens the battle: 'Then call we this the field of Agincourt, / Fought on the day of Crispin Crispianus.' The act of naming is the victory's first rhetorical monument. The place-name is not yet a battle-name; Henry makes it one. Then follows the Welsh-leek exchange with Fluellen — 'I am Welsh, you know, good countryman' — in which Henry publicly affirms his Welshness (he was born at Monmouth) and binds himself to the Welsh soldiery whose longbows had carried the day. This is Henry at his rhetorical best: naming the victory, crediting God, binding the army. It is also the moment the play's highest civic music sounds. The play does not deflate it here; the Epilogue will.
sources: Peter Saccio, Shakespeare's English Kings (2nd ed., 2000)
Gwyn A. Williams, When Was Wales? (1985)

trials.prompt: What is the structural problem with Gower's interpretation at 4.7 lines 4–8?
options: Gower retroactively interprets Henry's prisoner-killing order as retaliation for the French raid on the boys — but in the text Henry gave the order BEFORE the raid was reported. The play keeps both framings visible, neither adjudicated

trials.prompt: What parallel does Fluellen draw between Henry and Alexander the Great?
options: Alexander drunk killed his friend Cleitus; Henry sober rejected Falstaff — the parallel suggests the rejection was a moral action of the same kind. Delivered in Welsh-accented dialect, this is one of the play's most damning critical observations about its king

trials.prompt: Why does Henry's exchange with Fluellen about the leek matter (lines 85–98)?
options: It publicly affirms Henry's Welshness (he was born at Monmouth) and binds him to the Welsh soldiery whose longbow archers won the battle — a rhetorical moment of unity after naming the victory 'Agincourt'
